entities.name: Careerspan
entities.context: V's company; domain where this career strategy position applies

entities.name: Market Fundability
entities.context: A candidate's ability to be 'funded' or hired by the market, determined by perceived risk

entities.name: Professional Acceptability
entities.context: How acceptable a candidate appears for a role based on career trajectory

entities.name: Geometric Distance
entities.context: Measure of how far a career move is from one's established career core

entities.name: Career Core
entities.context: An individual's established anchor of competence (domain + role combination)

entities.name: Degrees of Freedom
entities.context: Simultaneous changes in domain and role during a career pivot

entities.name: Information Asymmetry
entities.context: Gap between what a candidate knows about their abilities and what an employer can verify

entities.name: Single-Axis Shift
entities.context: Career move changing only one variable (role OR domain) while keeping the other constant

entities.name: Multi-Axis Shift
entities.context: Career move changing multiple variables simultaneously, removing the anchor

entities.name: Sequential Vectors
entities.context: Career strategy approach treating moves as a series of single-variable changes over time

relationships.context: Market fundability is a function of geometric distance from career core
Professional acceptability is a function of geometric distance from career core
Each additional degree of freedom increases information asymmetry
Single-axis shifts allow the career core to anchor the new variable
Multi-axis shifts remove the anchor, maximizing information asymmetry
Sequential vector strategy maintains market trust and fundability
Careerspan coaching methodology applies sequential vector career strategy
